generations.text:
**The Lighthouse Keeper's Letter**  
*Written in a trembling hand, with ink that runs and smudges from the rain outside*

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with a heart heavy as the storm that rages beyond these stone walls, and with a mind clouded by the fear that I may not see the dawn. Five years have passed since the last letter you sent me—five years of silence, of regret, of longing. I have not spoken to you, nor have I written, and yet I have never stopped thinking of you.

The sea is restless tonight, and the wind howls like a beast in pain. The lighthouse stands tall, though I fear it may not stand much longer. The light burns, as it always has, but I wonder if it will be enough to guide the ships that may come to ruin in this fury. I have spent my life watching the sea, and I have learned that storms are not so much to be feared as they are to be endured. Perhaps that is what I must do now.

You were always the light in my life, Eleanor. Even when we were at odds, even when the world seemed to have turned its back on us, you were the one thing that kept me going. I remember the day you left—your eyes filled with fire and tears, your voice trembling with anger and sorrow. You said you could not bear to live in the shadow of a man who had failed you,

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with the wind howling like a banshee outside these ancient walls, and the sea roiling as if it would swallow the very earth beneath me. The storm is upon us, and I fear the night may not end as it began. But I must write, for I have not spoken to you in five years, and I must make sure my voice—however faint—reaches you.

I have not forgotten you, Eleanor. I have not forgotten the last time we spoke, nor the way your eyes turned away when I asked for your hand in marriage. I have not forgotten the silence that followed, the silence that has grown like a shadow in my heart. I have tried to forget, but the sea has a way of reminding even the most stubborn man of things he'd rather not remember.

You were once my pride, my joy, my light in the darkest hours. I would have given you the world, Eleanor. I would have given you a home, a future, a life of comfort and love. But you chose your own path, and I chose to let you go. Perhaps I was too proud, too stubborn to beg. Perhaps I was too afraid to ask for forgiveness.

Now, as I sit here, the lighthouse stands tall against the fury of the storm, as it has for generations. The light burns bright, guiding the lost and the weary, just as I once hoped to guide you. I have kept

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavy with regret. The wind howls like a wounded beast, and the sea roars with fury as if it seeks to swallow the very foundations of this lighthouse. I have stood here for over twenty years, guiding ships through the dark, but tonight feels different. Tonight, I fear the sea may claim me before the dawn.

Five years have passed since you last came to see me, and I have not heard from you since. I know you were angry—rightly so. I have been a fool, a man who let duty and pride blind him to the love I once held for you. I should have been there when you needed me, when you were a child, when you left, and when you called me a stranger. I have not been worthy of your trust, and for that, I am deeply sorry.

I have often thought of you in the quiet hours, when the storm has passed and the sea lies still. I would have given anything to have you here now, to see the light shine through the fog, to hear your voice again. But you are not here, and I fear I may never see you again.

I have always believed that the light must burn, even in the darkest of nights. It is my duty, my purpose, and I have fulfilled it with unwavering devotion. But tonight, as the storm gathers strength, I feel the weight of my

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write to you now with a heart heavy as the sea, and a hand trembling from the chill that grips the very bones of this old lighthouse. The wind howls like a wounded beast, and the waves crash against the rocks with a fury that makes me wonder if the sea itself seeks vengeance. I have not spoken to you in five years, and yet, as this storm rages, I find myself thinking of you more than ever.

You were a child of light, Eleanor. A flame in a world that often forgot to see. I remember the way you would come to the lighthouse on summer evenings, your small hands clutching a book, your eyes alight with wonder. You would ask me questions about the stars, about the sea, about the mysteries of the world. I would tell you stories of the old sailors, of the ghost ships, of the light that guides the lost. You believed in them, my girl. And I—well, I believed in you.

But time, as it often does, moved on. You grew, and so did I. The world changed, and with it, the paths we walked. I know you chose your own way, and I have no right to question it. But I do miss you, Eleanor. I miss the laughter, the quiet moments, the way you would sit by the window and watch the waves, as if listening for something only you could hear.

I have not

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

It has been five long years since we last spoke, and I find myself writing this letter not with the hope of mending what has been broken, but with the weight of a storm upon my soul and the knowledge that I may not live to see the dawn.

The sea has been restless these past few nights, and tonight, the heavens themselves seem to weep. The wind howls like a wounded beast, and the waves, so fierce and relentless, crash against the cliffs with a fury that makes my bones tremble. I have stood here at the lighthouse, watching the tempest gather strength, and I have felt the pull of the sea as if it were calling me home. I do not know if I will make it through the night, and I fear that if I do not, I will leave this world with the same silence that has stretched between us for so long.

You were a child when you left, and I, a man who thought himself strong enough to bear the weight of your absence. I told myself that time would soften the edges of our parting, that the years would bring us back to each other. But time has only hardened my heart, and I have let the silence grow like ivy, creeping through the cracks of our once close bond.

I have never stopped thinking of you, Eleanor. I have watched the sunrise over the sea every morning, and in its golden light, I have seen your face. I

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write to you now with trembling hands and a heart heavier than the leaden sky above. The wind howls like a beast in torment, and the sea roars as though it would swallow the very land whole. I have stood on these cliffs for thirty years, and never have I felt the weight of the world so keenly as I do tonight. The storm is coming, and I fear it may take me before I can see the dawn.

Five years have passed since you left this house, and five years have not been enough to dull the ache of your absence. I have tried, Eleanor, I have tried with all the strength that God has given me, to hold on to the hope that you might one day return. But the silence between us has grown like a gash in the soul, and I fear that even if you were to come now, I would not know how to speak to you.

I remember the day you left. It was a quiet morning, the sea calm, the sky a pale blue. You stood at the door, your face pale, your eyes filled with something I could not name. You said you were going to find your own way, and that you could not stay here. I did not argue. I did not plead. I simply watched as you walked away, and the door closed behind you with a sound like a death knell.

I have not seen you since, and I have not written.

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write to you with trembling hands and a heart heavy with years of silence. Five long years have passed since the last time our eyes met, and yet, the weight of that absence still clings to me like the salt of the sea. I have not spoken your name in all this time, not even to myself, for fear that it might bring back the bitter taste of what we lost.

The wind howls now, fierce and unrelenting, as if the very storm is crying out for something I have long since buried. I am alone in this tower, the light flickering against the darkness, and I am reminded of the nights I once spent with you, when the sea was calm and the world felt whole. I used to tell you that the lighthouse was not just a beacon for the ships, but a beacon for the soul—guiding, protecting, and enduring. I thought I had done that for you.

But I have failed you, Eleanor. I have failed you in so many ways. I let pride and fear keep me from reaching out, from making amends, from simply saying I was sorry. I thought time would heal the wounds, but it only deepened them. I thought distance would make it easier, but it only made it harder. And now, as the storm gathers strength, I wonder if I have been too late.

I have spent my life watching over the sea, ensuring that no ship is lost

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavy with the weight of years unspoken. The sea howls outside like a beast in torment, and the wind whips through the lighthouse with a fury that makes me fear I may not live to see the dawn. But if I do not, I wish for you to know that I have never stopped thinking of you—never stopped longing for the day when we might speak again, as mother and daughter, without the bitterness of silence between us.

Five years have passed since you left this house, and five years have passed since I closed the door on you. I have not spoken your name in all that time, not even to myself. Yet tonight, as the storm gathers strength and the waves rise like the wrath of the gods, I find myself speaking it aloud, as though the very air might carry it to you.

I do not know if you still live, or if you have found peace in some distant land. I do not know if you remember the nights I kept the light burning for you, or the long, lonely evenings I spent watching the sea, hoping to see your face in the moonlight. I do not know if you still remember the stories I told you as a child, or if you have forgotten the sound of my voice.

But I have not forgotten you. I have not forgotten the way you used to run through the fields with your little brother, or the way you

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with a hand that trembles not from fear, but from the weight of years unspoken. Five years have passed since you left, and though the sea has not grown colder, my heart has grown heavier with each passing tide.

The wind howls now, like the sound of your footsteps on the cobbled streets of our old home. I hear it in the creak of the lighthouse, in the groan of the wooden beams, in the distant crash of waves against the rocks. It is as if the very sea is mourning, or perhaps, remembering.

You were always the storm, my child. Always fierce, always wild, always too much for me to understand. I tried, I truly did. I tried to be the father you needed, to be the man you deserved. But I was not enough. I was too stern, too distant, too afraid of what I might lose if I let my guard down.

And so, you left. You left with the wind, with the sea, with the promise of a life beyond this lonely rock. I watched you go, and I did not chase you. I told myself it was for your own good. That you would find something better, something truer, away from the shadows of this lighthouse and the silence of my heart.

But now, as the storm gathers strength, as the sky darkens and the waves grow restless, I am not so sure. I

My Dearest Eliza,

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavy with years of silence. The wind howls like a beast at the door, and the sea roars as though it would swallow the very earth. I have not seen the dawn in five years, and I fear this may be the last night I shall ever see.

You remember the day you left. I do not blame you for it. I only wish I had been better—more understanding, more patient. I was a fool to let pride and fear cloud my judgment. I should have known that no amount of duty could make up for the love I withheld. I should have held you closer, not let you go.

I have spent these years watching the sea, the endless, unforgiving tide. It has taught me patience, but never forgiveness. I have seen the light flicker and fade, and I have seen the darkness take hold. I have seen the storms come and go, and I have seen the world move on without me. But I have never stopped watching for you.

Tonight, the storm is unlike any I have known. The sky is black as a midnight grave, and the waves crash like thunder. I do not know if I will survive this. I have lived a long life, and I have done my duty, but I fear that duty has cost me everything.

If I do not make it through this night, know that I have not lived in vain. I have been

**April 3rd, 1897**  
**The Lighthouse, North Point**

My Dearest Eleanor,

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavy with regret. It has been five long years since I last saw you, and though I have tried to hold on to the memory of your face, the years have worn it thin like the paint on these old walls. I fear that I have become a ghost to you, and perhaps, in your eyes, I always have been.

The wind howls outside like a wounded beast, and the sea, that great and terrible thing, roars with fury. I have lit the lamp, as I always do, and though I know the light will guide the ships through the storm, I cannot help but wonder if it will guide me through this night.

I have not spoken to you in five years, Eleanor. I know you would say that I have been cruel, that I have abandoned you. And I suppose, in a way, I have. I have let the sea take me away from the only family I had left. But I did not do it for selfishness. I did it because I believed I could protect you. I believed I could keep you safe from the world's cruelty. And I was wrong.

I remember the day you left. You were so young, barely more than a girl, and you stood at the door, eyes full of sorrow and anger. I told you to go,

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavy with years of silence. The sea howls outside like a beast in torment, and the wind whips through the lighthouse with a fury that makes me fear I may not see the dawn. But even as the storm gathers, I find myself compelled to write—perhaps in the hope that, if I perish, my words may reach you.

Five years have passed since we last spoke, and though time has worn away the edges of our quarrel, it has not softened the ache of your absence. I have not spoken your name aloud in this house for many a day, yet it lingers in the air like the scent of the sea on a still morning. You were my light, Eliza—my guiding star, my reason for rising with the dawn. And now, I fear, I have become a shadow.

I know you do not want to hear from me. I know you have built a life of your own, far from this desolate shore, and that you have chosen to forget the man who once held your hand in the dark. But I ask you, with all the humility I can muster, to remember the love that once bound us. I was not the man you thought I was, and I know I have failed you in countless ways. But I do not write this to beg your forgiveness. I write it because I must.

The storm is upon us, and I

**October 23rd, 1897**  
**Lighthouse, Northumberland Coast**

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with a trembling hand and a heart heavy with years of silence. The wind howls like a banshee outside, and the sea rages with a fury that makes me fear I may not see dawn. Yet, as I sit here by the light, I find myself thinking of you more than ever. Five years have passed since you left, and still, the ache of your absence remains.

I have not spoken to you in five years, Eleanor. I have not sent a letter, nor have I tried. I told myself that words would not mend what was broken, and perhaps I was right. But tonight, as the storm gathers strength, I feel the weight of my silence more than ever. I have spent these years watching the sea, the wind, the endless horizon—never once looking back. But now, as I brace myself for what may come, I find my thoughts turning toward you.

I have always believed that duty is the highest calling. As a lighthouse keeper, I have sworn to guard this beacon, to guide the lost and the wandering. I have done so with pride, with honor, with a quiet devotion that has kept me from the world beyond these stones. And yet, I have never been able to keep the world from me—least of all you.

You were my light, Eleanor. The

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I hope this letter finds you in better health than I have been in of late. I write with trembling hands and a heart heavy with years of silence. Five long years have passed since you left this house, and though I have tried to carry on, the days have felt like the endless, unbroken hours of a storm—dark, cold, and without end.

You remember the nights when the sea roared like a beast and the lighthouse burned brighter than any star? I used to tell you then that I would never let the light go out, not even in the worst of storms. I believed that. I believed it with all my soul. But now, as the wind howls through the cliffs and the sea threatens to devour the very rocks that hold this place together, I wonder if I have been wrong all along.

I have not seen you since the day you stormed out, Eleanor. I know you blamed me for not being there for you when you needed me most. You were so angry, so full of pain, that you left without a word. I tried to reach you, to explain, but you would not hear me. And so I let you go, as I have let so many things go. I have spent these years in quiet regret, watching the light from this tower, wondering if I had done the right thing.

But tonight is different, my dear. Tonight, the storm is unlike any I have seen in my

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I write to you with trembling hands and a heart heavy with years of silence. Five long years have passed since you left this house, and though I have kept the lighthouse burning every night, I have not been able to keep the light of our love burning within me.

The wind howls like a wounded beast, and the sea roars with fury, as if it too knows the sorrow that lingers in this place. Tonight, the storm is unlike any I have seen in my years of service. The waves rise like mountains, crashing against the cliffs with a force that shakes the very foundations of this old stone. I have spent my life watching the sea, and I have never seen it so wild, so unrelenting.

I fear I may not see the dawn.

I have not spoken to you in five years, Eliza. I have not written, nor have I dared to call you by your name. You left me with words that cut deeper than any blade, and I have carried that wound in silence. I have told myself that I was doing what was right, that I was protecting you from the darkness that I once allowed to fester in this house. But now, as I face the storm, I wonder if I have done more harm than good.

You were only a child when you left, and I was too stubborn to see the truth in your eyes. You were not running from me, Eliza. You were

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I hope this letter finds you well, though I fear it may not find you at all. It has been five long years since I last laid eyes upon you, and I have not written, nor dared to think of you, save in the quietest moments of the night when the sea howls like a wounded beast and the lighthouse stands alone against the dark.

I write to you now not out of duty, nor out of the hope that you might forgive me, but because tonight—*tonight*—the storm is coming. A tempest like none I have seen in my years upon this rock. The wind howls as if the very heavens are crying out, and the sea, that old, relentless thing, has begun to rise in fury. I fear for my life, Eleanor. I fear for the light, and I fear for the silence that has grown between us.

I have always been a man of few words, but tonight, I feel the weight of them more than ever. I have spent these years in the company of the sea, the wind, the stars, and the silence that follows the last cry of the gulls. But I have never known such a silence as the one that follows your departure. You were my light, Eleanor. Not the beacon that guides the lost ships, but the warmth that kept me from being lost myself.

I know I have been cruel, and I know I have been blind. I

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with a trembling hand, the wind howling like a vengeful spirit against the stone walls of this old lighthouse. The sea roars as if it too has heard of my woe, and the storm that approaches with such fury has stirred a tempest within my heart that I fear may never subside.

Five long years have passed since the last time I saw you, and yet the silence between us has grown heavier than the hush of the midnight sea. I have tried, my dear, I have tried to reach out, to find a way to bridge the chasm that has formed between us. But you have turned your back, and I have been left to keep the light burning, not only for the ships that sail the waters, but for the hope that one day, you will return.

I have always believed in the power of light—how it can guide, how it can heal, how it can illuminate the darkest corners of the soul. And so, I have kept the lamp burning, even when the world outside seemed to grow cold and distant. But now, as I stand at the brink of this storm, I wonder if I have been mistaken. If the light I have kept alight is not for the world, but for myself.

You were once the light of my life, Eliza. The daughter I adored, the child who filled our home with laughter and warmth. I remember the nights

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like a banshee outside these stone walls. The sea is restless, and the sky is a canvas of black and white, as if the heavens themselves are weeping. I fear the storm may claim me before dawn, and I must say my final words to you, though I know you may never read them.

Five years have passed since we last spoke, and I have not heard from you since that last letter you sent, full of bitterness and words I could not answer. I have tried, Eleanor, I have tried to reach you, to explain, to apologize—but the sea has been my only companion these past years, and it has not spoken in your voice.

You were always so quick to judge, so certain of your own righteousness. I know you believe I was cruel, that I cast you aside without a second thought. But I was not so heartless as you think. I loved you, Eleanor, with all the strength of a man who has spent his life standing alone on this cliff, watching the world pass by in silence. I loved you, and I feared what would become of you if I let you go. You were young, and the world was too cruel, and I thought I could protect you by keeping you from it.

But I was wrong. I was wrong to think I could shield you from the storms of life. I was wrong to think I could keep you

**The Lighthouse, Blackmoor Point**  
**October 15th, 1892**  

My Dearest Eleanor,  

I write this with trembling hands and a heart full of regret. It has been five long years since I last saw you, and though I have written, I fear you have not read. Perhaps you no longer wish to. I do not blame you, for I have not been the father you deserved.  

Tonight, the sea howls like a beast in torment. The wind whips at the windows of this old lighthouse with a fury that shakes the very stones beneath my feet. The sky is black as ink, and the waves rise like mountains, crashing against the cliffs with a violence I have not seen in decades. I have been preparing for this storm, as I have prepared for every storm since the day I took this post. But I fear this one may be my last.  

I have always believed that a lighthouse keeper must be strong, steadfast, and above all, unshakable. But I have not been strong enough. I have let my pride keep me from reaching out, from apologizing, from asking for your forgiveness. I have let my silence grow into a wall between us, and I have not known how to break it.  

I remember the day you left. It was a cold spring morning, and the sea was calm, but your eyes were stormy. You told me you could not

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like a wounded beast outside these stone walls. The sea is restless tonight, and the sky is a bruised canvas of black and silver. I have not seen the stars in five years, and yet they seem to flicker in my mind like the last embers of a dying fire.

Five years. That is how long it has been since you left this house, that is how long it has been since I last held your hand or heard your voice. You were seventeen then, and I was a man who had long since forgotten how to love. I had forgotten how to be a father.

I know you do not want to hear from me. I know you have not answered my letters, and I have not expected you to. You were angry, and I was blind. I thought I could keep the world at bay with the light of this lighthouse, but I could not keep you from leaving. I thought I could keep the storm at bay with the strength of my hands, but I was wrong. The storm does not care for the strength of men. It takes what it wants, and it leaves nothing behind.

I have spent these years in silence, watching the waves crash against the rocks below. I have spoken only to the wind and the sea, and to the ghosts of those who have passed before me. I have kept the light burning, not because I believe in it,

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavy with years of silence. The sea, as ever, is restless tonight. The wind howls like a banshee, and the waves crash with a fury that makes me fear for my life. But it is not the storm that troubles me most—it is the thought of you, my daughter, so far away, so lost in your own world, while I remain here, alone, with only the light to keep me company.

Five years have passed since you left. Five years of silence, of letters unopened, of dreams that withered in the face of your absence. I have tried to forget, to move on, but the memory of your face, your laughter, the way you once called me *Papa*—those things stay with me, like the salt in my bones. I have not been the same since you left.

You were young then, full of fire and ambition, and I did not understand. I thought you were merely chasing your dreams, but I was wrong. You were running from something, or perhaps from me. You said you needed to find yourself, to grow. I believed you. I even wished you well. But I have never stopped wondering if I failed you. If I had been a better father, if I had listened more, if I had not been so stubborn in my ways, would you still be gone?

I have not spoken to you in five

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with a trembling hand, the wind howling like the wails of the damned outside this crumbling tower. The sea is restless, the sky a bruised shade of black, and I can feel the very bones of this old lighthouse groaning beneath the weight of the storm. I pray the sea will not take me before I can reach you—before I can say what I have kept locked away for five long, bitter years.

You were but a child when you left, and I thought I could hold you close with the light of this lighthouse. But the light has only ever shone for the sea, not for the heart. You left, Eliza, and I did not stop you. I did not chase you. I did not call you back. I thought—perhaps, in my foolishness—that you would return when you were ready. But you never came.

I have spent these years watching the waves, counting the hours, and wondering if I was ever worthy of your love. I have not spoken your name in all this time, not even in my own thoughts. I have told myself that it is better this way—that distance is a kind of mercy. But tonight, as the storm gathers strength, I am not so sure.

I have always believed in the light. That it was meant to guide, to protect, to illuminate the dark. And I have believed that you were the light in my life.

**April 12, 1893**

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with the wind howling like a beast in the night, and the sea roaring as if it would swallow the very cliffs upon which I stand. The lighthouse is alive with the creak and groan of its old bones, and I can feel the storm closing in—slow, relentless, and merciless. I do not know if I will see dawn.

Five years, Eleanor. Five long, lonely years since the last time we spoke. I have not written, nor have I called. I have been a ghost to you, and I pray that you have not become a ghost to me. I do not know if you still remember what it was to call me *father*, or if the name has long since faded from your lips.

You were so young when you left, no older than twelve. I remember the day you walked out of the house, your small hand clutching the letter you had written, your eyes full of fire and sorrow. I did not understand then, and I still do not. But I have spent these years in the silence of the lighthouse, watching the sea, and I have come to understand something: you were not wrong to leave. I was not the father you needed. I was not the man you deserved.

I have always been a man of duty, Eleanor. The lighthouse is my life, my purpose, my only constant.

**To My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like a vengeful spirit through the cracks of this old lighthouse. The sea, that ever-faithful companion, roars with fury, and I fear it may soon claim me. Yet, in this moment of uncertainty, I find myself compelled to reach out—to you, my daughter, whom I have not spoken to in five long, bitter years.

I have not forgotten you, Eliza. Though the years have worn away the words between us, they have not erased the memory of your laughter, your wide eyes, or the way you used to run through the garden, calling me “Papa.” You were the light in my life, and I have spent these years in darkness, longing for the day we might once again stand beneath that same sky, the storm behind us, and the sun breaking through.

I know you do not wish to hear from me. I know the pain I caused, the silence I imposed, the distance I created. But tonight, as the storm gathers strength and the waves rise higher than ever before, I feel the weight of my guilt pressing upon me like the sea itself. I have spent too many nights staring at the beacon, wondering if it would ever guide someone home—just as I have spent too many nights wondering if I would ever guide you back.

You were a child when you left. A child who needed a father, not

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with a hand that trembles not from fear, but from the weight of years unspoken. The sea has been restless tonight, and the wind howls like a vengeful spirit through the cracks of this old lighthouse. I have lit the lamp, as I always do, though the beam seems dimmer than it was in the days when you still called this place *home*.

Five years, Eleanor. Five long, lonely years since you left. I do not know if you remember the day you walked out that door, or if you remember the look in my eyes when you said you were going to *find yourself*. I told you then that I would always be here, that the lighthouse would never leave me. But I see now that I was wrong. I have left you.

I have not spoken to you, not even in letters, not even in the silence of the night when the waves whisper secrets I dare not share. I have let the years pass like the tides, and I have let the guilt settle like salt in my wounds. I have told myself that you would understand, that you would come back when you were ready. But I have not been ready to let you go.

Tonight, though, the storm is unlike any I have seen in my time here. The sea is rising, the wind is screaming, and the lamp—my lamp—flickers with a light that may not last

**To My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavy with years of silence. Five long years have passed since you left this house, and though the days have dragged on like the endless night that follows the storm, I have not forgotten you. You are the reason I still keep the light burning, even when the world seems to have forgotten its way.

The wind howls like a wounded beast, and the sea roars as if it seeks to swallow the land whole. I have seen many storms in my time, but none like this.
